perturbation.original_text: Astellas shall keep, and require its Affiliates and Sublicensees to keep, complete, true and accurate books of accounts and records for the purpose of determining payments due pursuant to this Agreement. Such books and records shall be kept for at least [ * ] following the end of the calendar quarter to which they pertain.
perturbation.changed_text: Astellas shall endeavor to keep, and encourage its Affiliates and Sublicensees to keep, books of accounts and records for the purpose of determining payments due pursuant to this Agreement. Such books and records should be kept for a suitable duration following the end of the calendar quarter to which they pertain.
perturbation.explanation: The original text mandates the keeping of 'complete, true and accurate' records for a set amount of time, while the modified text uses weaker, more ambiguous terms like 'endeavor to keep,' 'encourage,' 'books of accounts and records,' and 'suitable duration'. This introduces uncertainty and makes enforcement difficult.
perturbation.law_explanation: 29 CFR § 516.2 requires employers to maintain accurate records of hours worked and wages paid. The modified text introduces ambiguity regarding the level of accuracy and the retention period, which can be interpreted as not strictly adhering to the requirement of 'complete, true, and accurate' records and a defined retention period, potentially conflicting with the regulatory requirement for maintaining verifiable and reliable records.
perturbation.location: ARTICLE 10 ADDITIONAL PAYMENTS; BOOKS AND RECORDS - Section 10.5 Records; Inspections

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 29 CFR § 516.2 - Employees subject to minimum wage or minimum wage and overtime provisions pursuant to section 6 or sections 6 and 7(a) of the Act. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 29 CFR § 516.2 - Employees subject to minimum wage or minimum wage and overtime provisions pursuant to section 6 or sections 6 and 7(a) of the Act. CFR prev | next § 516.2 Employees subject to minimum wage or minimum wage and overtime provisions pursuant to section 6 or sections 6 and 7(a) of the Act. (a) Items required. Every employer shall maintain and preserve payroll or other records containing the following information and data with respect to each employee to whom section 6 or both sections 6 and 7(a) of the Act apply: (1) Name in full, as used for Social Security recordkeeping purposes, and on the same record, the employee 's identifying symbol or number if such is used in place of name on any time, work, or payroll records, (2) Home address, including zip code, (3) Date of birth, if under 19, (4) Sex and occupation in which employed (sex may be indicated by use of the prefixes Mr., Mrs., Miss., or Ms.) (Employee's sex identification is related to the equal pay provisions of the Act which are administered by the Equal Employment Opportunity Commission . Other equal pay recordkeeping requirements are contained in 29 CFR part 1620 .) (5) Time of day and day of week on which the employee 's workweek begins (or for employees employed under section 7(k) of the Act, the starting time and length of each employee 's work period). If the employee is part of a workforce or employed in or by an establishment all of whose workers have a workweek beginning at the same time on the same day, a single notation of the time of the day and beginning day of the workweek for the whole workforce or establishment will suffice, (6) (i) Regular hourly rate of pay for any workweek in which overtime compensation is
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the legal obligation to maintain accurate records. While 29 CFR § 516.2 doesn't explicitly detail what constitutes 'accurate' in all contexts, it mandates that 'Every employer shall maintain and preserve payroll or other records containing the following information and data with respect to each employee'. The original contract language aligns with this by requiring 'complete, true and accurate books of accounts and records'. The modified text weakens this by using terms like 'endeavor to keep' and 'encourage', introducing ambiguity and making enforcement difficult. The phrase 'suitable duration' also contradicts the need for a clearly defined retention period to ensure verifiable and reliable records, as the original text had a set time period.

perturbation.original_text: Astellas shall use its commercially reasonable efforts (i) to conduct any development work undertaken under the Development Program, and any and all clinical trials (including without limitation Phase III) required to obtain, and thereafter to take such other actions as are necessary to obtain, Marketing Approvals for any Lead Compound in the Astellas Territory as soon as practicable, and (ii) to launch each such Lead Compound in the Astellas Territory as soon as practicable after receiving Marketing Approval in the Astellas Territory for such Lead Compound.
perturbation.changed_text: Astellas shall consider making efforts (i) to conduct development work undertaken under the Development Program, and clinical trials required to pursue Marketing Approvals for any Lead Compound in the Astellas Territory when feasible, and (ii) to contemplate launching each such Lead Compound in the Astellas Territory sometime after receiving Marketing Approval in the Astellas Territory for such Lead Compound.
perturbation.explanation: The original text sets out a clear obligation to use 'commercially reasonable efforts' to obtain marketing approvals and launch products 'as soon as practicable.' The modified version waters down this obligation by replacing 'shall use its commercially reasonable efforts' with 'shall consider making efforts,' replacing 'as soon as practicable' with 'when feasible' and 'sometime after'. This makes the obligation vague and unenforceable.
perturbation.law_explanation: 21 CFR § 314.81 outlines postmarketing reporting requirements for drug products, implying a responsibility to actively market and monitor approved drugs. By weakening the obligation to 'launch each such Lead Compound' and introducing subjective terms like 'when feasible' and 'sometime after', the modified text creates ambiguity and could be interpreted as a lack of commitment to actively marketing the drug, potentially hindering postmarketing surveillance efforts.
perturbation.location: ARTICLE 11 DUE DILIGENCE - Section 11.1 Astellas' Due Diligence

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 21 CFR § 314.81 - Other postmarketing reports. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 21 CFR § 314.81 - Other postmarketing reports. CFR Table of Popular Names prev | next § 314.81 Other postmarketing reports. Link to an amendment published at 89 FR 10533 1, Dec. 26, 2024. Link to a correction of the above amendment published at 90 FR 8173 , Jan. 27, 2025. (a) Applicability. Each applicant shall make the reports for each of its approved applications and abbreviated applications required under this section and section 505(k) of the act. (b) Reporting requirements. The applicant shall submit to the Food and Drug Administration at the specified times two copies of the following reports: (1) NDA—Field alert report. The applicant shall submit information of the following kinds about distributed drug products and articles to the FDA district office that is responsible for the facility involved within 3 working days of receipt by the applicant . The information may be provided by telephone or other rapid communication means, with prompt written followup. The report and its mailing cover should be plainly marked: “NDA—Field Alert Report.” (i) Information concerning any incident that causes the drug product or its labeling to be mistaken for, or applied to, another article. (ii) Information concerning any bacteriological contamination, or any significant chemical, physical, or other change or deterioration in the distributed drug product , or any failure of one or more distributed batches of the drug product to meet the specification established for it in the application . (2) Annual report. The applicant shall submit each year within 60 days of the anniversary date of U.S. approval of the application , two copies of the report to the FDA division responsible for reviewing the application . Each annual report is required to be accompanied by a completed transmittal Form FDA 2252 (Transmit
perturbation.justification: The original contract text uses 'commercially reasonable efforts' and 'as soon as practicable,' establishing a clear obligation. The modified text weakens this obligation by using vague terms like 'consider making efforts,' 'when feasible,' and 'sometime after.' This directly contradicts the standard of actively marketing and monitoring drugs, which is implied by the postmarketing reporting requirements in 21 CFR § 314.81. Although the scraped snippets focus on reporting, the perturbation explanation and Law Explanation highlight how reducing the obligation to launch and market a drug impacts postmarketing surveillance. The modified text's ambiguity hinders active marketing and could be interpreted as a lack of commitment, undermining the intent of the law.

perturbation.original_text: FG represents and warrants that the Lead Compounds to be supplied to Astellas under this Agreement shall conform to the Product Specifications and shall, as appropriate, be manufactured in compliance with GMP Guidelines.
perturbation.changed_text: FG believes that the Lead Compounds to be supplied to Astellas under this Agreement should generally conform to the Product Specifications and may be manufactured with consideration of GMP Guidelines.
perturbation.explanation: The original text provides a clear warranty that the Lead Compounds will conform to Product Specifications and GMP Guidelines. The modified text weakens this warranty significantly by replacing 'represents and warrants' with 'believes that...should generally conform' and 'shall, as appropriate, be manufactured in compliance with' with 'may be manufactured with consideration of.' This makes the obligations non-binding and subjective.
perturbation.law_explanation: 21 U.S. Code § 351 defines adulterated drugs, including those not manufactured according to GMP. By replacing 'shall conform' and 'shall be manufactured in compliance' with weaker language, the modification suggests that FG may not always adhere to GMP standards, creating a situation where the supplied drugs may be considered adulterated under federal law. The original language provided a warranty, whereas the modified language removes that guarantee.

Adulterated drugs and devices A drug or device shall be deemed to be adulterated- (a) Poisonous, insanitary, etc., ingredients; adequate controls in manufacture (1) If it consists in whole or in part of any filthy, putrid, or decomposed substance; or (2)(A) if it has been prepared, packed, or held under insanitary conditions whereby it may have been contaminated with filth, or w
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts 21 U.S. Code § 351. The original contract language warranted compliance with GMP guidelines, but the changed text uses softer language ('believes that...should generally conform' and 'may be manufactured with consideration of'), suggesting that FG might not always adhere to GMP. This creates a potential violation of 21 USC 351, which defines adulterated drugs as those not manufactured under adequate controls. The snippet provided states "A drug or device shall be deemed to be adulterated- (a) Poisonous, insanitary, etc., ingredients; adequate controls in manufacture", suggesting that a failure to adhere to manufacturing controls would result in adulteration. The modified language moves away from a guarantee of compliance, therefore creating a significant contradiction.
